resources.string: APK Editor needs to read the APK you pick and save the signed copy back to your device. On this Android version it uses the system file picker, so you can grant access there per file.
APK Editor needs storage access to read the APK you pick and to save the signed copy back to your device.
Skip — I\'ll use the system picker
Pick an APK and edit its package name, version, permissions, activities and other manifest settings through a simple form. Download a re-signed copy when you\'re done.

Can do: change package name, version, min/target SDK, app flags, permissions and activities — without unpacking the whole app, so it\'s fast.

Can\'t do: change app code, resources, icons or layouts. A package-name change rewrites the manifest only (not resources.arsc), so it works for many apps but isn\'t a guaranteed full clone.

Re-signing: the edited APK is re-signed with a stable key, so it installs as a separate or replacement app and won\'t receive Play Store updates. Re-editing the same app later produces a matching signature.

Uploads are processed on the live backend and auto-delete within 30 minutes — or immediately after you save.

The app\'s unique ID, like com.whatsapp. Change it to install a second copy alongside the original. Rules: letters, digits and underscores only, no spaces, at least one dot, each part starting with a letter. This rewrites the manifest only (and re-anchors the app\'s components to the original package), so it works for many apps but isn\'t a guaranteed full clone.
The internal whole number Android uses to compare versions — higher means newer. To update over a previous install, use a higher number than before. Digits only.
The version text shown to users, e.g. 1.2.3. Any text is allowed — it\'s just a label.
The display name shown under the icon. Editable only when it\'s plain text; if it points to a string resource (@string/…) it lives outside the manifest and can\'t be changed here.
The lowest Android version the app will install on, as an API level. Lowering it lets the APK install on older phones (the app may still crash if it actually uses newer features).
The Android version the app is built to target, as an API level. Affects some runtime behaviours and the permission model.
Leave blank if it\'s the same as the store password.
Leave blank to use the first key in the store.
Lets you attach a debugger and use adb run-as. Handy for testing; normally off for release builds.
Whether Android may include the app\'s data in backups and restore it.
Allow plain, non-HTTPS (http://) network traffic. Some apps need this.
Whether bundled native (.so) libraries are extracted to storage at install time.
Ask Android for a larger memory limit for the app.
Tick to turn on, untick to turn off. Flags you don\'t touch are left exactly as they were. Press left/right on a row to toggle.

exported = other apps and the system may start this screen. launchable = give it a home-screen icon (adds a MAIN/LAUNCHER entry and turns on exported). Untick to change a setting, or remove an activity entirely.
Removed activities won\'t exist in the rebuilt app.
The same key is used every time, so editing this app again later (same package, higher version code) installs as an update.
The server copy auto-deletes the moment you save it (and within 30 min regardless).
Choose a keystore file, or switch to the default key.
Check for a newer version on GitHub.
